summary: McKenzie receives his first feature request for Bingo Card Creator from a customer who needs custom header text on bingo cards. The feature doesn't exist yet and the customer needs it by tomorrow, making it impossible to deliver in time. Rather than leaving her without a solution, he researches a competitor's product, installs their trial to verify it works, and refers her to them. He reflects on the bittersweet nature of helping a potential customer while losing the sale.
key_points: Early-stage products inevitably face feature gaps that cost real sales
McKenzie prioritizes the customer's needs over his own revenue by referring her to a competitor
He validates the competitor's product himself before recommending it, showing thoroughness even in losing a sale
The incident reveals his product roadmap awareness — the feature was already planned but not yet built
money_quotes: I ended up pointing her to one of my competitor's products which appears to have the feature she needs (I even installed their trial to make sure, and it appears it will work for her).
This makes me happy-sad: happy I was able to give her at least an option, very sad it wasn't me.
key_insight: Helping a customer find the right solution — even from a competitor — is the instinct that builds long-term trust and reputation in a small software business.
